title: Product Innovation and Credit Market Disruptions
abstract: We provide new evidence that disruptions in firms’ access to credit during the Global Financial Crisis significantly affected product innovation in the consumer goods sector. We combine highly granular retail scan data with lending data and find that credit-constrained firms introduced fewer new products, those products were less novel, and new products sold less well. Overall, these findings suggest that disruptions to credit markets impair firms’ ability to compete for profits through new product offerings.

article.1. Data and Background:
JEL G21 - Banks; Depository Institutions; Micro Finance Institutions; Mortgages G31 - Capital Budgeting; Fixed Investment and Inventory Studies; Capacity G32 - Financing Policy; Financial Risk and Risk Management; Capital and Ownership Structure; Value of Firms; Goodwill L25 - Firm Performance: Size, Diversification, and Scope O31 - Innovation and Invention: Processes and Incentives O32 - Management of Technological Innovation and R&D Issue Section: Article Editor: Manju Puri Manju Puri Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Product innovation is key to firm growth. To compete in the marketplace, firms must continually update existing products and add new sources of revenue by introducing new products that expand their product portfolios. A firm’s ability to survive and grow is largely predicated on how well its new products preserve and broaden its customer base (e.g.,Klette and Kortum 2004;Aghion, Akcigit, and Howitt 2015;Kogan et al. 2017;Argente, Lee, and Moreira 2018a;Akcigit and Kerr 2018;Braguinsky et al. 2021). Although researchers have devoted considerable attention to the margins that affect firms’ entry, growth, and survival, much less is known about the factors that shape the process of product innovation over an economic cycle.Broda and Weinstein (2010)andArgente, Lee, and Moreira (2018b)find that economic downturns leave behind a missing generation of new products. These papers, however, provide little explanation of the mechanisms behind the reduced rate of new-product introduction during a recession. Perhaps firms hold back on introducing new products during a recession because they expect weak product demand as inShleifer (1986), or perhaps supply-side factors like disruptions to credit markets are to blame.

In the absence of financing frictions, the decision to innovate by introducing a new product should depend mostly on the expected value of the new product. The development and introduction of a new product, however, usually entails large up-front investments in research-and-development (R&D), physical capital, working capital, advertising, and product placement. Thus, when credit access becomes constrained, firms might be forced to halt the investments involved in creating new products, even if doing so entails a sacrifice in value. This paper sheds light on the relationship between credit access and product introduction by investigating whether disruptions in the supply of credit affected the quantity, novelty, and sales performance of products introduced in the aftermath of the Global Financial Crisis of 2008.

We examine these questions using a large data set from the Nielsen Retail Measurement Service that offers highly granular information about individual products. The data allow us to see when a firm introduces a new product to market, and we exploit this observation to measure whether the firm is innovating in the marketplace. The data cover a broad range of products in the consumer goods sector, which represents approximately 14%of total consumption of goods in the United States. Moreover, this sector spans a diverse cross-section of product markets ranging from food and nonfood groceries to general merchandise like small kitchen appliances.

We focus on the introduction of new products for three main reasons. First, we consider that it is important to better understand the motivations behind firms’ decisions to compete through new product offerings. Research on innovation and economic growth has long recognized the introduction of new products as the main vehicle through which innovation activity translates into growth (e.g.,Klette and Kortum 2004) and recent studies in the industrial organization field (e.g.,Wollmann 2018) have emphasized the critical role that new-product offerings play in defining the competitive equilibrium in a wide variety of sectors. Moreover, new products do not necessarily result from research breakthroughs that lead to new patents. For instance, firms might add new products to their portfolios because they want to compete for profits with design innovations that increase consumer appeal or because they want to expand their technical expertise and business know-how into new product markets (e.g.,Penrose 1955;Braguinsky et al. 2021). Yet, relatively little is known about how financial frictions affect firms’ decisions to compete for profits through the addition of new products.

Second, the process of bringing a new product to market does not end when a firm completes the basic research that leads to a patent filing. For such knowledge to come to fruition and become a new product that satisfies previously unmet consumer needs, firms must make large, up-front investments in product development, experimentation, physical and working capital, and product advertising and placement (e.g.,Horsky and Simon 1983;Thomke 1998;Argente et al. 2021b). Since these required up-front investments are incremental to the R&D costs behind a product, credit market disruptions could affect the margin of product expansion more than they affect the margin of new patent applications.

Finally, prior work has used patent applications as their main measure of innovation activity. Yet, because many firms do not use the patent system at all, patent filings fail to capture all innovative activity in the product market.Argente et al. (2021a)find that smaller, nonpatenting firms are responsible for the majority of new products introduced in the market andGraham et al. (2018)indicate that only 6%of firms in the manufacturing sector use the patent system at all. The Nielsen data set’s comprehensiveness represents an advantage because smaller, younger, and nonpatenting firms tend to account for much of an economy’s dynamism (e.g.,Decker et al. 2014;Adelino, Ma, and Robinson 2017), but these firms also tend to be more credit constrained. Our focus on product introduction using the Nielsen data set, therefore, allows us to take these smaller firms into account and better measure the aggregate impact of credit market shocks in the process of product creation across the entire economy.

Following existing work byAkcigit and Kerr (2018)andBraguinsky et al. (2021), we distinguish between new products that firms add to theirexistingproduct lines and new products thatexpandfirms’ product portfolios into new product lines.1Bringing a new product to market within a firm’s existing product lines will likely require a smaller investment; such a product is developed with existing business and technical expertise and existing capital can more easily be redeployed for production. In contrast, products that expand a firm’s portfolio into new product lines represent bolder moves because they require greater investments in R&D capabilities, in product development and experimentation, and in new physical capital for production. We support these assertions with evidence that the introduction of such products is more likely to be preceded by an expansion of the number of plants that the firm operates. These products, though more expensive to develop than new products within a firm’s product lines, are also less likely to cannibalize on the performance of a firm’s existing products and tend to pay off with greater firm growth.

We focus our attention on disruptions to the credit market that emanate from the market for external bank financing. Banks are a crucial source of financing for firms of all sizes and ages.Robb and Robinson (2014)and evidence from the Survey of Business Owners show that entrepreneurs extensively rely on bank debt to finance start-ups and manufacturing expansions. Thus disruptions to external bank financing following the financial crisis plausibly curbed consumer-goods firms’ ability to invest in the development and introduction of new products.

We develop two measures of a firm’s exposure to the credit market disruptions that followed the Global Financial Crisis. Our first measure followsGreenstone, Mas, and Nguyen (2020)to exploit preexistinggeographicvariation in firms’ exposure to banks that significantly reduced their aggregate supply of small business loans in the period 2007–2010. This measure builds on the shift-share approach popularized byBartik (1991)and offers the advantage of covering a broad sample of firms regardless of whether data about their capital structure and external financing needs are publicly available. This measure, however, does not capture individual firm’s needs for external financing and is also most relevant for smaller firms, who predominantly access finance through local banks (e.g.,Granja, Leuz, and Rajan 2022).2

Our second measure of credit access addresses these limitations. FollowingAlmeida et al. (2012),Costello (2020), andBenmelech, Frydman, and Papanikolaou (2019)we usefirm-levelvariation in the need to raise external funds during the financial crisis. Specifically, we hand-match the Nielsen data set to the Loan-Pricing-Corporation (LPC) syndicated loans data set. We then compute the percentage of each firm’s long-term syndicated debt that matured in the early stages of the financial crisis. This measure is useful because firms that had a high proportion of debt coming due at the beginning of the crisis were forced to refinance during a period in which credit spreads widened considerably (e.g.,Santos 2010) and bank lenders rationed many of their borrowers. Therefore, these firms would be more credit-constrained than others during the financial crisis. This measure covers a much smaller sample of larger firms but, in return, gives us a clearer indication of whether these firms needed external financing during the crisis.

We find that credit market disruptions are associated with a significant decline in the rate of product introduction, which is largely accounted for by a reduction in the rate at which firms introduce new products in “new-to-the-firm” product lines. Our empirical results are similar when using either measure of credit market disruption, even though the two measures use distinct sources of variation and have distinct samples. A conservative back-of-the-envelope calculation suggests that credit market disruptions may account for between 15%to 20%of the aggregate decline in the introduction of new products in new product lines. Our results are consistent withKrieger, Li, and Papanikolaou (2022), whose study of the pharmaceutical sector shows that positive wealth shocks encourage “bolder” product innovations that carry both greater risks and greater rewards.

We further exploit cross-sectional heterogeneity across firms and products to clarify whether our results are consistent with the credit market friction channel. This analysis shows that our results are more pronounced in subsamples of younger and smaller firms and in firms whose main product lines are in sectors that rely more on debt to finance investments (e.g.,Rajan and Zingales 1998). Consistent with the idea that semidurable products are more capital-intensive and thus more sensitive to financial frictions than nondurable products, we find that our results are stronger when we focus on the subset of firms that produce semidurable products. Overall, these cross-sectional exercises support our conjecture that the decline in the introduction of new products to “new-to-the-firm” product lines is related to disruptions in the supply of credit during the crisis.

We address concerns that both our measures of credit market disruptions might be capturing firms’ exposure to unobserved shocks unrelated to the supply of credit. For instance, if a bank specializes in lending to a specific industry or market (e.g.,Paravisini, Rappoport, and Schnabl 2015), a demand shock to that industry might look like a shock to the supply of credit because this bank would reduce lending across all counties relative to other banks in the same counties. We address this concern and other concerns by exploiting the Nielsen data to ensure that our findings are robust against controls for unobservable time-varying industry demand shocks. We further test the analysis by using alternate definitions of the dependent variable and alternate geographic- and firm-level measures of credit market disruptions, applying additional controls for local demand shocks, and running our main empirical specifications on a subsample of firms whose sales are dispersed across a large number of states. Each of these alternate strategies returns the same results.

Using the descriptions of each new product in the Nielsen data, we then evaluate whether credit market disruptions affected the degree of novelty of the new products introduced during the Global Financial Crisis. We followArgente and Yeh (2017)andArgente et al. (2021a)to compute novelty indexes for all the products that a firm introduced during each year. These indexes measure the novelty of a new product’s characteristics relative to the characteristics of all products ever sold within the same product line.

We find that products introduced by credit-constrained firms during the crisis period were less novel than those introduced by unconstrained firms during the same period. To ensure that this decline in new-product novelty is not explained by firms having cut back on introducing low-value novel product characteristics, we document that credit market disruptions are associated with a decline in products’ novelty indexes even when we weigh a product characteristic using its “shadow price” obtained from a hedonic-pricing regression of (log) prices on product characteristics. These results suggest that credit market disruptions are partly to blame for a missing generation of new products and for a missing generation of novel product characteristics.

Finally, we exploit the granularity of the data to ask whether credit market disruptions affected products’ commercial performance, conditional on their introduction. The role that credit market constraints play in shaping the outcomes of new products launched in new product lines during a downturn is not obvious. On one hand, credit market frictions could negatively affect investments in the development and promotion of products which could, in turn, limit the products’ appeal, diffusion, and commercial success. On the other hand, constrained firms might concentrate their efforts on their very-best new products during an economic downturn, focusing on products with the most consumer appeal.

We use an empirical specification that includes fixed effects at the product line×cohort level to provide evidence that products introduced by credit-constrained firms in new product lines during the crisis period generated 20%less revenue. This specification compares the sales performance of new products introduced by credit-constrained firms with the sales performance of new products introduced by less-constrained firms in the same product line and during the same quarter. We further condition on firm fixed effects to ensure that the results are not driven by systematic differences in the characteristics of firms that introduced new products in new lines during the crisis. We interpret our results as being in accord with those of others who have shown that tight credit markets constrain the growth of new firms entering the economy (e.g.,Ates and Saffie 2021;Moreira 2016;Siemer 2019).

To better understand how credit market constraints affect new products’ ability to generate sales, we further decompose the sales from each of a firm’s new product lines. Specifically, we decompose the total sales of a new product line into the number of products carried in that product line (scope), the number of designated market areas (DMAs) in which the firm sells the products in the new product line, and the number of retail chains that carry these products. We then ask whether the credit market disruptions’ impact on sales comes from a relatively more-limited geographic, retail chain, or product scope or from the lower average sales of each product in the new product line. Our findings suggest that both margins are relevant. The negative association between credit market disruptions and average sales per product accounts for most of the effect on total sales, but credit market disruptions also negatively affect the number of products that a firm introduces in a new product line and the number of retail chains in which the new products are sold.

Our results thus far raise a broader question about the channels through which the credit market disruptions affect firm behavior. Are credit market disruptions bringing R&D of new products to a standstill? Or are firms delaying the launch of fully researched and developed new products until they have the financial ability to invest in these products’ production and promotion? Alas, our data set does not allow us to directly observe what products werenotintroduced during the crisis period, whether their introduction was abandoned or simply delayed, or what stage of development they were in. We are able, nevertheless, to offer exploratory analysis that provides some insight. In supplemental analyses presented in theInternet Appendix, we find that credit market disruptions were negatively associated with the filing of new patent applications during the crisis period. This finding suggests that the impact of credit market disruptions goes beyond a delay in the timeline for the introduction of products whose R&D is already complete and that the crisis might also have affected the pipeline of new products that firms have available to bring to market. Our results further suggest that credit market disruptions affected primarily the introduction of products in new product lines, which require greater up-front investments and also affected investments in product distribution even among the products that were actually launched, suggesting that firms may have also halted the roll-out of their products because of difficulties in financing the later stages of the product introduction cycle. These results tie together the evidence in our paper and suggest that all phases of the process of launching a new product were likely disrupted by the Global Financial Crisis.

The paper closest to ours is that ofNanda and Nicholas (2014), who document that credit frictions originated by bank distress during the Great Depression affected the amount and quality of new patents filed by firms. We think that our results add to those ofNanda and Nicholas (2014)in at least two dimensions. First, we focus on a different time period and financial crisis. The innovation landscape changed significantly in the 80 years that passed between the Great Depression and the Global Financial Crisis. For example,Babina, Bernstein, and Mezzanotti (2020)document that the Great Depression induced a reallocation of innovation activities from individual inventors operating outside firm boundaries to research centers within firms. Because individual inventors are less resilient to credit market disruptions than firms, the relation between financial frictions and product innovation could have fundamentally changed. Thus, our paper suggests that despite structural changes to how innovation works, credit market disruptions still significantly affected the rate and quality of product introductions during the Global Financial Crisis. Second, our focus on the introduction of new products allows us to shed light on the broader debate about how credit market shocks affect firms’ strategic decisions to compete through nonprice strategies, such as new product offerings, in addition to our contribution to the literature on innovation.

Our paper relates to an important literature at the intersection of financing policy, international trade, and industrial organization.Chevalier (1995),Phillips (1995),Chevalier and Scharfstein (1996),Campello (2003)and more recently,Kim (2021)andHyun and Kim (2018)investigate how capital structure and financial frictions affect firms’ pricing decisions and overall market performance.Phillips and Sertsios (2017)examines how financial flexibility shapes firms’ responses to consumer demand shocks.Kabir (2019)finds declines in consumer welfare as a result of reductions in product offerings during the Global Financial Crisis.Hellmann and Puri (2000)andFracassi, Previtero, and Sheen (2022)investigate how private equity financing and venture capital affect elements of nonprice competition, such as firms’ introduction of new product offerings or the time that startup firms need to bring products to market. Using survey data from Russia,Bircan and De Haas (2020)finds that deeper credit markets foster investments in product innovation andMendes (2019)examines how credit-supply shocks affect the product mix and production cycles of the Portuguese wine industry. Using granular trade data sets,Paravisini et al. (2015)andFriedrich and Zator (2020)examine how companies adapt their product mixes and export markets following an economic shock. Our findings complement existing evidence that firms compete more aggressively on prices in response to credit market shocks. They suggest that credit market disruptions impaired firms’ ability to invest in new product offerings during the crisis which could have relaxed competition through nonprice strategies.

Finally, the paper also fits into a broad literature examining how capital structure and financing constraints shape innovative activity. A number of papers (e.g.,Brown, Fazzari, and Petersen 2009;Acharya and Xu 2017) suggest that entrepreneurs and firms primarily rely on internal cash flows and equity markets to finance R&D investments.3Other papers (Benfratello, Schiantarelli, and Sembenelli 2008,Kerr and Nanda 2015,Hochberg, Serrano, and Ziedonis 2018, andMann 2018), however, point to bank financing as important drivers of R&D investments and innovation.Bolton, Chen, and Wang (2011)andBolton, Chen, and Wang (2013)predict that credit market shocks lead to cuts in investment andHuber (2018)andDoerr (2021)find that firms and entrepreneurs exposed to cuts in bank lending file fewer patents.4Howell (2017)andKrieger, Li, and Papanikolaou (2022)find that R&D subsidies and positive shocks to net worth encourage firms to innovate and affect both the type of innovation and the success of such innovations. We contribute to this literature by using a highly granular data set with a comprehensive coverage of firms in an important sector in the economy to provide evidence of how credit market disruptions following the Global Financial Crisis affected both the type of new products brought to market and the quality and overall success of those products.5
Our product data come from the Nielsen Retail Measurement Services (RMS) scanner data set provided by the Kilts-Nielsen Data Center at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business. The Nielsen RMS scanner data set is collected from POS systems in retail stores. Weekly, each individual retail location reports the sales and quantities of every product sold in the store. The data consist of more than 100 billion unique observations at the UPC×store×week level covering approximately $2 trillion in sales. These sales account for a very large fraction of all sales in retail locations in the United States.6The data are collected from more than 40,000 distinct stores in around 90 retail chains in 371 metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) and 2,500 counties. The data set thus provides great coverage of the universe of products and of the full portfolio of firms in the consumer goods sector.

The original data cover more than one million distinct products identified by UPC. Nielsen organizes the products into a hierarchical structure that is illustrated inFigure 1. Each UPC is classified into 1 of 1,070 product modules, which are organized into 104 product groups, and these are then grouped into 10 major departments.7For example, a 31-ounce bag of Tide Pods (UPC 037000930389) is mapped to product module “Detergent-Packaged” in product group “Detergent,” which belongs to the “Nonfood Grocery” department. We identify the timing of a product’s introduction by observing the first sale of the product in the data set. More specifically, we define product entry as the first quarter in which a product was sold.Internet Appendix Aprovides a more detailed discussion of the product data set and construction of the sample of products.

To compute firm-level measures of product introduction and credit market disruptions, we link products to firms using information obtained from GS1 US, the single official source of UPCs. The GS1 US data include all UPC prefixes, which are specific to each company. We combine these prefixes with the UPC codes in the Nielsen RMS data set to link firms to barcodes.8With this link, we can observe a firm’s portfolio of products in the consumer goods sector at any point in time, which lets us determine whether and when a firm launched a new product.

To better illustrate the advantages of combining the GS1 and Nielsen data sets,Figure 1charts the product portfolio of a firm that produces medical equipment. By combining these data sets, we can match each barcode sold by XYZ Corp. to the producer and we can track the evolution of XYZ’s product portfolio over time. For instance, XYZ Corp. originally focused on the gauze pads and cotton swabs product lines. With the combined data set, we can see that during the sample period, XYZ not only grew its gauze pads product line with the new product Drain Sponges but also introduced the new product “Swabsticks flavored with Dentrifice”, that expanded the firm’s portfolio into a new product line (the “Dental Accessories” product module within the “Oral Hygiene” product group).

Moreover, the combined data set allows us to measure the sales, price, and quantity of each product in a firm’s portfolio, including its new products. This information allows us to follow how sales of each new product evolve; a feature of the data difficult to replicate using other measures of innovation like patents. Finally, the GS1 data include information about the names and geographic characteristics of firms. We use this information to match the firm-product portfolio to other data sets that in turn allow us to measure the exposure of each firm to credit market disruptions.

We measure exposure to credit market disruptions at the county-level using the Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council’s (FFIEC) Community Reinvestment Act (CRA) data set of small business lending. The data set contains information about the amount and number of small business loans originated by each reporting financial institution in each county of the United States during a calendar year.9All commercial and savings banks with total assets exceeding $1 billion are required to report their originations of small business loans by the county of the borrower. According toGreenstone, Mas, and Nguyen (2020)andNguyen (2019), CRA-eligible banks issue approximately 86%of all loans originated in this market, which mitigates concerns that the sample is not representative of the market.

To compute our firm-level measure of credit market disruptions, we link the GS1 data set with loan-market data from the Thomson-Reuters DealScan database. DealScan collects detailed information about syndicated loans and the identities and characteristics of respective borrowers and lenders from a variety of sources including regulatory filings, media reports, and company briefs.

We link the DealScan and GS1 data sets using a multistep merging procedure. First, we merge the two databases using exact company names and location information. Second, we match the remaining companies in the product database to the Thomson Reuters DealScan database using a bigram string comparator for the company name, while requiring the state of the company to be the same. We keep all matched pairs that are headquartered in the same state and have a name-matching score greater than 0.97. Finally, we hand-match the remaining companies in the GS1 database with names that exactly match those of the DealScan database but that list different addresses.10
article.2. Measuring Credit Market Disruptions:
Our analysis is focused on credit disruptions that emanate from the market for external bank financing. Prior studies have found that bank financing is a crucial form of financing especially for younger and smaller firms.Petersen and Rajan (1994)show that smaller firms benefit from bank relationships, andRobb and Robinson (2014)document that newly founded firms primarily rely on external debt as their main source of financing and increasingly so as they mature.11

We measure firms’ exposure to credit market disruptions using two distinct approaches. We begin by using geographic variation in the severity of local credit-supply shocks in the county in which each firm is headquartered. This approach allows us to measure credit market frictions for a broad sample of firms because the measure does not require any information about a firm’s financials and capital structure. The geographic measure will capture variation in the health of banks located near firms; however, it gives no direct information on a firm’s need for external financing. The geographic measure is also most applicable to smaller firms whose access to bank credit is mostly local.12To address these limitations, we use an alternative firm-level measure that captures preexisting variation in the fraction of each firms long-term debt that matured during the financial crisis. This measure is a better indicator of external financing needs but covers a smaller sample of generally larger firms. Below, we will explain the computation of these measures in greater detail.

2.1 Geographic measure of credit market disruptions

The aggregate amount of small business loans originated in the United States declined significantly between 2007 and 2010. Panel A ofFigure 2shows that the total amount of small business loans went from a peak of $320 billion to less than half of that amount in just 3 years. This aggregate decline in small business lending conceals substantial heterogeneity in the changes in small business lending across U.S. counties between 2007 and 2010. Panel B ofFigure 2shows that more than 25%of all counties saw declines in small business lending greater than 60%⁠, whereas approximately 5%of U.S. counties saw an increase in small business lending over the same period. The intensity of the decline, however, conflates cuts in credit supply with lowered demand for external financing from local businesses.

Panel B is a histogram of the county-level percentage change in total county small business lending between 2007 and 2010. Our first approach to isolating a firm’s exposure to disruptions in credit supply followsGreenstone, Mas, and Nguyen (2020). The idea is to use preexisting variation in banks’ market shares across counties to compute a measure of each county’s precrisis exposure to banks that registered significant contractions in small business lending during the financial crisis at a national level. Intuitively, this measure exploits the notion that some banks (e.g., JP Morgan Chase, Citibank) significantly cut back on their originations of small business loans relative to the rest of the banking sector and that some counties were more exposed to such banks through the banks’ preexisting branch footprint.13

This approach hinges on the assumptions that small business lending is inherently local and that borrowers cannot easily replace local bank relationships with financing from other sources (e.g.,Petersen and Rajan 1994;Berger and Udell 1995;Nguyen 2019). These assumptions are plausible for the small- and medium-sized firms that comprise the majority of the Nielsen sample.Granja, Leuz, and Rajan (2022)show that more than 75%of all small business loans are originated between borrowers and lenders located in the same county.14

To compute their measure of local credit market disruptions, Greenstone, Mas, and Nguyen (2020) develop a modified Bartik approach that refines the traditional approach by exploiting the granularity of the CRA small business lending data set. In line with Greenstone, Mas, and Nguyen (2020) , we use the CRA data to estimate the following equation: Δ S B L b , c 07 − 10 = γ b + δ c + ϵ b , c , (1) where the outcome variable is the percentage change in small business lending originated by bank b in county c between 2007 and 2010, γ b are bank fixed effects, and δ c are county fixed effects. The measure of exposure to credit-supply shocks at the county level is then computed as S B L S h o c k c = − ∑ b ( γ b ^ × s b , c 07 ) , (2) where s b , c 07 is the share of small business loans originated by bank b in county c during 2007. Note that unlike the traditional Bartik approach, Greenstone, Mas, and Nguyen (2020) use the bank fixed effects estimated from Equation ( 1 ) rather than each bank’s aggregate change in lending to construct the measure of local exposure to shocks. The difference lies in how the county fixed effects in Equation ( 1 ) purge each bank’s aggregate change in lending from the bank’s exposure to unobservable demand shocks in the counties where they are located, thus addressing potential concerns that the measure could capture the bank’s exposure to local demand shocks instead of shocks to the local credit supply.

A potential concern with the modified Bartik approach is that the bank-supply and firm-demand parameters might not be well-identified if the world is better described by a more-general class of models in which the change in small business lending is determined by Δ S B L b , c = γ b + δ c + γ × Z b , c + ϵ b , c , (3) where Z b , c is a bank-firm interaction term. This case could obtain if some banks specialize in specific industries. Industry-specific demand shocks might then be identified as credit-supply shocks because the bank specializing in such industry would reduce its lending in all counties relative to other banks. In Internet Appendix C , we employ a battery of empirical tests that evaluate this specific concern and we confirm that our results are not sensitive to alternative specifications that take this concern directly into account.

The strategy ofGreenstone, Mas, and Nguyen (2020)only guarantees that shocks to banks are orthogonal to contemporaneous local demand in the case that bank lending can be described by an additively separable decomposition into bank supply and firm demand (e.g.,Amiti and Weinstein 2018). Thus, we cannot entirely rule out the possibility that channels other than credit supply drive our results. To help in convincing the reader that we are measuring the effects of credit-supply disruptions, we employ an alternative measure that exploits a very different source of variation.

2.2 Firm-level measure of exposure to credit market disruptions

The geographical measure of credit market disruption described above covers all firms in our sample but gives no direct information about whether firms needed external financing during the crisis. This section describes our alternative approach for measuring each firm’s need for external financing during the Global Financial Crisis.

This alternative approach closely follows that ofAlmeida et al. (2012)and others (e.g.,Benmelech, Frydman, and Papanikolaou 2019;Costello 2020), who use preexisting variation in the fraction of long-term debt that was scheduled to mature at the beginning of the financial crisis. The approach builds on the well-documented notion that though the seeds of the recent crisis were sown in the subprime mortgage market, the troubles later spilled over to the corporate debt market in the fall of 2007. A series of papers (e.g.,Ivashina and Scharfstein 2010;Almeida et al. 2012;Chodorow-Reich 2013) shows that banks significantly cut back their supply of corporate loans from their levels at the peak of the boom. These cuts were related to runs by short-term creditors and to problems emanating from other parts of the banks’ loan portfolios. The fraction of a firm’s debt that matured at the beginning of a crisis captures whether or not a firm was already scheduled to refinance their existing debt during the 2007–2008 period. Firms that had to refinance during this period likely experienced greater financial constraints in doing so, as credit risk spreads widened (Santos 2010) and credit markets became highly illiquid (Ivashina and Scharfstein 2010).

We followCostello (2020)to compute the fraction of syndicated loans that matured at the beginning of the financial crisis. More specifically, we compute the total principal amount of each firm’s syndicated loans that were initiated before July 2006 and were scheduled to mature between July 2007 and August 2008. Using similar maturity and loan initiation requirements, we also compute each firm’s total amount of outstanding debt as of July 2007. The ratio between the outstanding debt scheduled to mature between July 2007 and August 2008 and the outstanding debt as of July 2007 is our firm-level measure of credit market constraints. The maturity restrictions that we impose on the sample of loans used in the analysis ensure that firms’ ability to obtain long- versus short-term debt does not drive the results.

The critical identification assumption made in this analysis is that the fraction of syndicated lending that matured during the crisis is orthogonal to shocks in the expected value of investing in new products during the crisis. By using the fraction of loans that matured during the financial crisis, we attempt to tease out variation in financial constraints that is plausibly unrelated to systematic differences in the expected outcomes of firms’ products under development or in firms’ exposure to demand shocks. This approach comes with the caveat that higher-quality firms may be more likely to proactively manage risk by extending the maturities of their syndicated loans (e.g.,Mian and Santos 2018). Our findings, therefore, might be driven by unobserved differences in firm quality, which would be correlated both with the likelihood that a firm had a loan mature during the 2007–2008 period and with the expected value of introducing a new product during the crisis. We attempt to mitigate such concerns by assessing the robustness of our main results to a tighter matching of treatment and control firms. For instance, in robustness tests reported inInternet Appendix Cwe include product line×year fixed effects that exploit only variation across firms that specialize in the same type of products and ensure that the results will not be driven by systematic differences in the share of long-term debt maturing across product lines that have different sensitivities to the economic cycle.15

In sum, while we cannot entirely dismiss alternative explanations for our results, by using two measures of credit market disruptions that cover very different samples and use different sources of variation we are more confident we can capture the effects of credit market disruptions rather than unobservable factors that are correlated with the treatment status.
article.3. Credit market disruption and product:
This section documents the main empirical patterns in the relationship between credit market disruptions and the rate and characteristics of product introduction before, during, and after the financial crisis.

Our empirical strategy is largely predicated on the existence of a meaningful distinction between a firm introducing a new product within its product lines and that firm introducing a new product in a product line that is new to the firm. In particular, we conjecture that new products added to one of the firm’s existing product lines carry not only fewer investments and risks but also fewer rewards. Product introductions in a new product line, on the other hand, will likely require greater investments but will also result in greater growth opportunities (e.g.,Braguinsky et al. 2021) and less cannibalization of existing products (e.g.,Argente, Lee, and Moreira 2018a).18

Panel B ofFigure 3plots the average entry rate of new products in existing product lines, and panel C plots the average entry rate of new products that expanded firms’ product lines. These plots offer initial evidence that credit market disruptions play a more-significant role in the slowing of firms’ introduction of new products in new-to-the-firm product lines. Panel B suggests that the entry rates of new products within firms’ existing product lines are not too different across the entire period for both groups. Consistent with the idea that an expansion to new product lines requires greater up-front investments and financing needs, panel C suggests that credit constraints play an important role in explaining the decline in the entry rate of new products in new-to-the-firm product lines. The average entry rate of new products in new-to-the-firm product lines declined by more than 45%between 2007 and 2010 for the more credit-constrained firms, but declined by only 25%for the less constrained firms.19

3.2 Empirical results

This section formally examines whether credit market disruptions induced changes in the entry rate and novelty of new products during the crisis. We estimate the following ordinary least squares (OLS) specification for several outcomes related to the introduction of new products as a function of the firm’s exposure to credit market disruptions: Y i , t = α i + θ t + β S h o c k i × C r i s i s t + Γ X i , t + ϵ i , t , (4) where i indexes a firm during year t . The dependent variable Y i , t represents a firm’s outcome during the year, either the entry rate of new products or the novelty of the year’s new products. The variable, S h o c k i × C r i s i s t ⁠ , is the interaction between a measure of credit market disruption, which could be the geographic- or firm-level measure described above, and a dummy variable that takes the value of one for the crisis years of 2008, 2009, and 2010. Our main coefficient of interest, β ⁠ , measures the impact that an increase in our measures of credit market disruption had on product introduction outcomes during the crisis.

Our main specification also includes firm and year fixed effects. The firm fixed effects ensure that our results are driven by within-firm changes in the decisions to introduce new products instead of by cross-sectional differences in invariant firm characteristics.20The year fixed effects absorb overall trends in the evolution of product introduction rates over time. In some specifications, we further include a vector of firm-level characteristics that includes the natural logarithm of the firm’s total sales, the Herfindahl index of revenue concentration across a firm’s products, the natural logarithm of average firm sales per product, and dummy variables for each decile of the distribution of the number of products in a portfolio. We winsorize the dependent variables at the 99%level to attenuate the effects of outliers. All regressions are estimated with heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors clustered at the level of the state.

Columns 1 and 2 present results using the entry rate of all products as the main dependent variable. The main coefficient of interest,β⁠, indicates that an increase in a firm’s exposure to local financial institutions that cut back their aggregate supply of small business lending is associated with a lower overall rate of product introduction. The results are statistically significant if we include the additional control variables employed in column 2.

Next, in columns 3–6, we decompose the entry rate of new products into products added within existing product lines (columns 3 and 4) and products that expand the scope of firm’s product portfolio to new-to-the-firm product lines (columns 5 and 6). The results in columns 3 and 4 suggest that an increase in our geographic measure of credit market disruption is associated with declines in the rate of new products within existing product lines, but these declines are not statistically significant. The results in columns 5 and 6 suggest that an increase in a firm’s exposure to credit market disruptions had a statistically significant impact on the rate at which firms introduce new products that broaden the set of product lines that a firm produces.

Because our geographic measure of credit market disruption cannot assess whether a specific firm needed external financing, we repeat the analysis using the firm-level measure of credit market disruptions. Panel B ofTable 3reports the results of this analysis. We find that firms with a larger share of their debt that matured during the financial crisis were significantly more likely to cut back on their rate of introduction of products in new-to-the-firm product lines (columns 5 and 6), but their rate of introduction of new products within existing product lines was unaffected (columns 3 and 4). The coefficients in columns 5 and 6 indicate that firms that refinanced all outstanding debt during the crisis reduced their entry rate in new-to-the-firm product lines by 3.5 percentage points. These empirical findings are reassuring in that we find similar empirical patterns even when we use an alternative proxy that covers a different subset of firms and picks up on a very different source of variation in credit market disruptions.

To address the possible concern that our measure of credit market disruption captures shocks to local demand and economic conditions that could be correlated to our main variable of interest, we repeat the analysis ofTable 3in a subsample of firms whose sales are not concentrated in their headquarters’ home state. These firms should be exposed to local credit shocks because their bank financing is likely local (e.g.,Petersen and Rajan 1994;Granja, Leuz, and Rajan 2022;Nguyen 2019), but not as exposed to local demand shocks as their sales are sourced from a broad geographic footprint through relationships with regional or national distribution channels.

The results presented in panel A ofTable 4reveal that the impact of credit market disruptions on the introduction of products in new-to-the-firm product lines is not sensitive to restricting the sample to firms that make more than three-quarters of their sales outside their home state. In panels B and C of the same table, we show that the results are also robust to further restricting the sample to firms that sell in more than 25 states (panel B) and that have an above-median level of geographic dispersion of sales across states (panel C).

InInternet Appendix C, we conduct a battery of robustness tests to further ensure that our headline findings are driven not by unobservable local or industry shocks, but rather by the impact of credit market disruptions on product introduction. In particular, we repeat our main empirical specifications after conditioning on more granular product line×year fixed effects and after including controls for local economic conditions and local demand shocks in the aftermath of the Global Financial Crisis. Our main results are not sensitive to these alternative specifications.

We further implement other robustness tests to gauge the sensitivity of the main results to alternative definitions of the dependent variables and alternative measures of credit market disruptions. For instance, we assess the robustness of our results to an alternative definition of new product that is based on the introduction of new brands rather than on new barcodes. We discuss the results of these and other empirical analyses inInternet Appendix D. Overall, the results are consistent with the idea that credit-constrained firms disproportionately cut investments in riskier projects.

3.2.2 The role of credit market disruptions: cross-sectional heterogeneity

To further validate the idea that credit market disruptions distort firms’ decisions to introduce new products, we investigate whether the impact of credit shocks during the crisis period varies based on firm and product characteristics that are typically associated with greater financing needs or with difficulties in accessing credit.

InTable 5, we examine the role that a firm’s age and size play in how it is affected by credit market disruptions. A large literature that started withPetersen and Rajan (1994)andBerger and Udell (1995)suggests that smaller and younger firms are more likely to be credit constrained as they lack established lending relationships and strong credit histories. We interact our main variable of interest with indicator variables that take the value of one if the firm is old and if the firm is the upper quartile of the firm-size distribution.21The results in columns 1 and 2 suggest that the average product introduction rates in new product lines of old firms were significantly less affected by the local credit shocks than those of new firms. In columns 3 and 4, we find that the product introduction rates of larger firms are also not as affected by credit market disruptions.22This result is consistent with the idea that smaller firms are more credit constrained, although we must note that the difference is not statistically significant in this analysis.

We also exploit the heterogeneity in product characteristics across the broad spectrum of products in the data set. Finally, we examine whether the effects of credit market disruptions on product introduction were more pronounced for firms in sectors that are more dependent on debt to finance their investments. We base our analysis on the idea that the introduction of new products should be more affected by credit shocks in firms whose sectors are more dependent on debt to finance growth. To measure each sector’s dependence on debt, we use a measure based onRajan and Zingales (1998)and compute the average (at the three-digit standard industrial classification level) of the ratio between net debt issuances and capital expenditures. In columns 7 and 8 ofTable 5, we find that the effect of credit market disruptions is more pronounced in sectors that depend more on debt to finance their capital investments.26

Overall, the results discussed in this section support our conjecture that the credit-supply channel is responsible for the association between our measure of credit market disruptions and the decline in the introduction of new products in new-to-the-firm product lines.

3.3 Credit market disruptions and product novelty

In the previous section, we determined that firms were less willing to expand into new-to-the-firm product lines during the crisis. Next, we will explore whether greater exposure to a credit market shock also induces changes in the qualitative characteristics of all new products introduced during the crisis period. We focus on a critical element of product innovation: the degree of novelty of a new product. This step examines whether the new products that credit-constrained firms introduce in a crisis are more likely to be “me-too” products that fail to bring novel characteristics to the marketplace.

We followArgente and Yeh (2017)and compute product novelty indexes using detailed information about the characteristics of each UPC in the Nielsen RMS data set. These product novelty indexes measure whether the characteristics of a new product are distinct and unique at the time of its introduction relative to those of all other products ever sold within the same product line.

We use three different measures of product novelty. Our first measure counts the number of unique characteristics in a new product and divides that count by the number of product attributes that Nielsen covers in that product’s respective product line. Our second measure slightly modifies the previous measure to consider the case in which a new product introduces a never-before-seen combination of characteristics even though no product characteristic is novel in itself. These two novelty indexes face the potential pitfall that they simply count novel characteristics or novel combinations of characteristics with no regard for how consumers value these novel product characteristics. To overcome this possible criticism, we followArgente et al. (2021a)in creating a novelty index that weighs each characteristic by its shadow price, obtained from regressions of (log) prices on a set of product attributes and a sequence of time dummies. We refer readers toInternet appendix Bfor further details on the computation of these measures.

To further understand the role of credit market disruptions in shaping this decline in product novelty during the crisis, we estimate the specification of Equation (4) using the novelty indexes as our dependent variables. We report the results inTable 6. Panel A shows results using the full sample of firms and our geographic measure of credit market disruption, and panel B reports similar findings using our DealScan-matched data set with our firm-level measure of credit market disruption. The results suggest that when firms are more exposed to credit market disruptions during periods of crisis they introduce new products that are less novel than the products they would introduce during normal periods. The results of columns 1 through 4 in both panels suggest that credit market disruptions negatively affect product novelty regardless of whether we are measuring novelty as never-before-seen product characteristics or as never-before-seen combinations of characteristics. Columns 5 and 6 confirm our results are robust to using the hedonic novelty index. This result suggests that the missing product characteristics that were cut during the recession were not those with lower consumer value.

article.4. Credit Market Disruptions and Product:
The recent literature in industrial organization has increasingly emphasized the important role that new product offerings play in determining the competitive equilibrium in many industries. For instance,Wollmann (2018)shows that accounting for the competitive decisions of incumbent automakers to introduce new models is important to evaluate the effects of mergers in this industry. Using the Nielsen data,Hottman, Redding, and Weinstein (2016)show that heterogeneity in product appeal is a key driver of heterogeneity in firm size, andArgente, Lee, and Moreira (2018a)show that firms must continuously update their product portfolios to avoid being outcompeted by their rivals’ own product improvements. The evidence we presented in the previous sections suggested that credit market disruptions could affect firms’ ability to compete and grow by limiting their ability to introduce new product offerings. Yet, despite greater exposure to financial constraints, some firms could decide to launch new products anyway. This could happen for a multitude of reasons, such as the need to reshape the product portfolio after some products lost their appeal and turned obsolete, to respond to competitors’ moves, or even to expand and diversify their sources of revenue. In this section, we evaluate whether credit market disruptions could also affect firms’ strategies and ability to compete for profits through their impact on the sales performance of the new product lines.

One of the main advantages of the Nielsen data set is that it offers an opportunity to track the sales generated by each new product over time. The role that credit market disruptions play in shaping the outcomes of new product lines launched during a downturn is not obvious. Credit market disruptions could limit investments in the development and promotion of new products, and the lower initial investments in product development could stunt both the products’ initial performance and future growth potential. On the other hand, during a downturn, the opportunity cost of investment in product introduction and development is higher, which could prompt firms to focus investments on their most-promising products. This selection effect would imply that products introduced by firms exposed to credit market disruptions during a crisis could perform better than products introduced in normal times, as they would be more promising to begin with.

To study these questions, we follow the evolution of sales of each firm in each product line over time. 27 This data set allows us to identify when an existing firm expands its offerings into a new product line and, crucially, to observe the sales that a firm generates in each of its product lines. To shed more light on the link between credit market disruptions and the relative performance of products introduced during the crisis period, we estimate the following specification: R e v i , g , c = α i + θ g c + β S h o c k i × C r i s i s C o h o r t c + ϵ i , g , c , (5) where R e v is a measure of the cumulative total sales that firm i generated in product line g launched during period c in its first four years of operation. This specification thus measures total sales performance over the initial years that a firm offers products in a new product line. The main variable of interest, S h o c k i × C r i s i s C o h o r t c ⁠ , is the interaction between the geographic measure of credit market disruption and a dummy variable that takes the value of one if firm i launched the new product line g between 2008:Q3 and 2010:Q4. The product line × cohort fixed effects, θ g c ⁠ , ensure that we are comparing the sales performance of new products introduced by different firms within the same product line during the same quarter. Thus, we are comparing product lines that face very similar economic conditions at inception but systematically differ in terms of their firms’ exposures to credit market disruptions. The firm fixed effects, α i ⁠ , ensure that the results are not driven by a composition effect whereby firms launching products in crisis periods are systematically different from firms introducing products in normal times.

Table 7gives the results. In column 1, we report the results of estimating the specification of Equation (5) using the natural logarithm of the total sales generated by the product line during its first four years as our dependent variable. Our data set includes only product lines that did not yet exist at the beginning of the sample. The results suggest that a one-standard deviation increase in our measure of exposure to credit market shocks is associated with a 20%reduction in the total sales generated by a new product line. In column 2, we repeat the analysis after restricting the sample to new product lines launched by incumbent firms. The results are not sensitive to the exclusion of the product lines launched by new firms.

A potential concern about the above analysis is that the new product lines of credit-constrained firms are underperforming because the entire firm is underperforming. To address this concern, we repeat the empirical analysis using the share of sales of the firm that is accounted by the new product line. In columns 3 and 4, we find that product lines introduced during the crisis by firms exposed to credit market disruptions account for a smaller share of firm revenues over their first 4 years. This result indicates that credit market disruptions negatively affect the performance of new product lines relatively more than they do the performance of the firm as a whole. Overall, these results agree with those of other papers (e.g.,Ates and Saffie 2021;Siemer 2019), suggesting that differences in initial economic and financial conditions strongly affect initial investment and explain the variation in the average size of start-up firms across time.

To further enhance our understanding of the above findings, we attempt to investigate what are some of the potential channels that shape the negative relation between credit market disruptions and sales performance of new product lines. Namely, we examine whether credit-constrained firms roll out fewer product varieties in their crisis-cohort product lines, whether they sell these crisis-cohort products in fewer geographies, or even whether they invest less in securing a large network of retailers. To that effect, we decompose the total sales generated by a new product line into (a) the average number of products sold within the product group and average sales per product; (b) the average number of designated market areas (DMAs) where products are sold and average sales per DMA; and (c) the average number of retail chains that carry the products and average sales per retail chain.

Panels A and B ofTable 8report the results of this analysis. In columns 1, 3, and 5, we estimate the impact of credit market disruptions on the average sales per product, per DMA, and per retail chain, respectively. The estimated coefficients suggest that the negative association between credit market disruptions and these variables accounts for most of the observed negative association between credit supply shocks and total sales performance. We interpret this result as consistent with the notion that products introduced by credit-constrained firms during the crisis period have less appeal than other products introduced by less-constrained firms in the same product group during the same period.28The results of column 2 in both panels suggest that credit market disruptions also negatively affect the number of products that a firm introduced in a new product line. This pattern might indicate that credit constraints limit firms’ ability to invest in multiple product varieties within a product line. Column 4 indicates that credit constraints are associated, albeit nonsignificantly in panel A, with less extensive availability of new product lines in terms of geography. Finally, in column 6 of both panels, we document a significant negative association between credit-supply shocks and the number of retail chains that carry new product lines launched by firms exposed to credit-supply shocks.

article.5. Discussion of Results and Economic Ch:
In this section, we provide an overall discussion and interpretation of the results reported in the paper.Broda and Weinstein (2010)andArgente, Lee, and Moreira (2018b)have previously documented a significant decline in the rates of product introduction during the Global Financial Crisis but do little to explore the reasons behind this decline. Our focus in this paper is in understanding whether our empirical results could in part be explained by the credit market disruptions that originated from the weak financial health of the banking sector. Our findings suggest that frictions in credit markets played a part in the slowdown in product innovation but were not the only driver of such slowdown. For instance,Figure 3shows that, during the 2008–2010 period, even the quartile of firms that was least exposed to our measure of credit market disruptions introduced about 25%fewer products in new product lines, suggesting that other forces, such as reduced consumer demand, contributed to the decline in product introduction rates (e.g.,Mian, Rao, and Sufi 2013).

This discussion naturally raises the question of how much of the decline in the introduction of new products was due to credit market disruptions. Our main results suggest that credit market disruptions are not strongly associated with the decline in the introduction of new products in existing product lines but are a statistically significant determinant of the introduction of new products in new product lines. What percentage of the overall slowdown in the introduction of new products in new product lines can be accounted by credit market disruptions?

To answer this question, we formally aggregate our cross-sectional estimates and evaluate the total loss of new products caused by exposure to credit market disruptions. We followMian and Sufi (2012)andBerger, Turner, and Zwick (2020)in exploiting differences in cross-sectional exposure and treating the group that suffered the smallest shock as a counterfactual. We present aggregate estimates of the impact of credit market disruptions under two distinct baseline scenarios. One that uses the bottom decile of exposure to local credit market shock as the counterfactual group and another baseline scenario of no local credit market shock. Furthermore, we take into account that our main effects are likely to be heterogenous across the firm-size distribution and that large national companies, such as Proctor & Gamble, L’Oreal, or ConAir Corporation, are unlikely to be affected by local credit market frictions. Thus, we compute our estimates under two baseline scenarios after excluding the top 1%⁠, 5%⁠, and 10%of the largest firms in the sample, respectively.

For each decile group, g , we compute the aggregate loss of new products in new product lines as: N e w P r o d u c t s g = β × ( S B L S h o c k g − ( B a s e l i n e E x p o s u r e ) ) × P r o d u c t g , 2007 , (6) where β is our preferred reduced form estimate from column 6 of Table 3 , S B L S h o c k g is the average exposure of each decile group to the credit market disruption shock with − 0.095 being the average exposure of the bottom group. P r o d u c t g , 2007 is the total number of products of firms in each group prior to the Global Financial Crisis after excluding the largest firms in the sample.

Table 9presents the estimates. Using the bottom decile as a counterfactual and excluding only the top percentile of firms in the firm-size distribution, we estimate that credit market disruptions decreased the number of new products in new product lines by approximately 1,138 per year. By comparison, firms in our sample introduced approximately 2,123 fewer products in new product lines per year in 2010 than they did in 2007. This estimate suggests that credit market constraints account for approximately 53%of the total decline in product introduction rates. This is likely an aggressive estimate because the bottom decile of firms was located in counties that received a positive exposure to our local credit market disruption measure. A more conservative approach is to compute our estimates relative to a baseline of zero exposure to the credit-disruption measure. When we use this baseline, our estimate drops to 571 new products in new lines lost or 27%of the decline in the number of new products. When we exclude a larger fraction of the right tail of the distribution of firm size, the estimates yield a range of products lost between 221 and 650 representing between 10%and 30%of the overall decline in the number of new products in new product lines between 2007 and 2010. Our preferred estimate averages the more conservative and more aggressive approach and excludes the top 10%of firms in the sample yielding an estimated impact of credit market disruption of approximately 15%of the overall decline in new products in new product lines.29

Average of A and B − 855 (40.2 % ⁠ ) − 489 (23 % ⁠ ) − 327 (15.4 % ⁠ ) This table summarizes our estimates of the aggregate effect of credit market disruptions on the introduction of new products in new product lines. We present estimates based on the coefficients obtained from the empirical analysis of Table 3 . The aggregate effect is presented based on different aggregation assumptions. In scenario A, we choose the bottom decile group as the counterfactual scenario. The estimates in this scenario are relatively more aggressive because the bottom decile group had an average positive local credit market shock. In scenario B, we employ a no-exposure baseline whereby the baseline local credit market shock is set to zero and decile groups with a positive local credit market shock are assumed to have lost zero products. We include estimates that account for firm-size heterogeneity in the effect of credit market disruptions by assuming zero treatment effects for larger firms that fall on the top 1 % ⁠ , 5 % ⁠ , and 10 % of the firm-size distribution in our main sample. The percentage impacts in parentheses are computed relative to an overall peak to trough decline of 2,123 new products in new product lines.

The estimates represent the number of products in new product lines lost on an annual basis. If we take our preferred estimate, credit market disruptions were possibly responsible for the loss of half-a-year worth of new product innovations in new product lines over the 3 years of the crisis.30We note thatFigure 3suggests that the gap between the high- and low-credit-shock group closes after 2010 despite product introduction rates returning to pre-2008 levels only during 2012. This pattern is consistent with evidence fromGopal and Schnabl (2022)that financial intermediaries other than banks, like fintech companies, stepped up to substitute for credit shortfalls in the local markets most affected by disruptions in the traditional banking sector.

We acknowledge that the limitations of our data set preclude us from digging deeper into the channels through which credit market disruptions affect firm behavior. We cannot observe which products were not introduced during the years of the Global Financial Crisis, whether they were abandoned or put on hold, or what stage of development were they in when the crisis unfolded. However, regardless of the stage of development abandoned projects were in, firms would have had to devote significant internal resources to move ahead with their introduction. InInternet Appendix E, we discuss evidence suggesting that the process of product introduction is complex and requires investment in multiple stages. There, we show suggestive evidence that financial frictions disrupted foundational investments in R&D activities as well as investments in plant capacity and marketing, all of which are necessary to bring a product to market.
article.6. Conclusion: Recent theoretical models in the Schumpeterian tradition increasingly model the process through which R&D investments lead to improvements of existing products that allow firms to keep their edge over competitors or in the development of new products that expand a firm’s product portfolio and displace competitors’s products (e.g.,Klette and Kortum 2004;Akcigit and Kerr 2018;Argente, Lee, and Moreira 2018a). We conducted an initial empirical investigation of the role that credit market disruptions during the financial crisis played in shaping the rate of new-product introduction, the novelty of those products, and their sales performance. We find that credit market disruptions did not affect the rate at which an incumbent firm introduced new products in its own product lines, but did constrain a firm’s expansion into new product lines. This fact carries important implications for firm and aggregate economic growth:Akcigit and Kerr (2018)estimate that 54.5%of aggregate growth due to innovations is accounted for by incumbent firms’ investments in new products outside their current scope of operations, whereas only 19.8%of aggregate growth from innovations is due to investments in internal innovation efforts. In the context of the results inAkcigit and Kerr (2018), our empirical findings suggest that credit market disruptions affect the margin of innovation that most contributes to growth. Our findings uncover a new channel through which credit market disruptions may cause long-run consequences to firm growth.
